title: AMI TS3007b degraded functional design meeting for TV remote
expected_context: The meeting is a functional design meeting for the remote-control project.
Each participant presents recent individual work and design recommendations.
Management guidance says the remote should be TV-only, internet should become the main focus, younger users should be targeted, and the company slogan/logo should be more recognizable on the remote.
The audio is degraded, so wording may be imperfect, but the core design direction and follow-up actions should be captured.
expected_decisions: Keep the remote focused on television use only, not as a general multi-purpose remote.
Target the new remote mainly at younger users, especially the sixteen-to-forty-five audience, while keeping it usable for older users.
Keep the most important controls, especially channel switching, volume, and teletext, clear and easy to access.
Prioritize simplicity: fewer visible functions, fewer buttons, and important actions available directly.
Stick primarily with infrared transmission and avoid adding Bluetooth, radio, or a receiver unless cost and compatibility justify it.
Avoid overbuilding the remote with too many gadgets or complex functions.
Consider touchscreen or LCD-style extra-function areas, but keep primary controls visible and usable.
Use the right-hand design option after the team vote.
Avoid a fragile pop-open or flip-open mechanism because it may be sensitive or easy to break.
expected_requirements: The remote should support TV use only.
The design should stay relatively cheap and simple.
The most-used buttons should be bigger, clearer, or more prominent.
Channel switching, volume control, and teletext should work quickly and directly.
Advanced or less important options should be hidden, placed away, or moved to menus rather than blocking primary controls.
The remote should give feedback when a user presses a button.
The remote should fit comfortably in the hand.
The company logo or slogan should be considered in the physical/aesthetic design.
The design should account for current TV technologies and compatibility limitations.
Session notes, minutes, or board outputs should be saved into the shared/project documents.
expected_research_findings: The marketing questionnaire involved about one hundred respondents.
The younger audience is the larger target group, while the older audience is smaller but still relevant.
Younger users are more interested in new technologies such as LCD screens and speech recognition.
Older users are less interested in new technology features.
Seventy-five percent of users find many current remote controls ugly.
Eighty percent of users would spend more money on a remote that looks fancy.
The most important or frequently used functions are channel switching, teletext, and volume controls.
Many buttons on current remote controls are not used.
Current remotes are mainly infrared, which helps cost and compatibility.
Bluetooth or radio would require compatible receiving hardware and may increase cost.
expected_design_ideas: A placeholder or docking place near the TV for storing the remote.
A locator feature where saying something like where is the remote makes the remote beep.
One button could support multiple minor functions.
Important buttons could be larger or more prominent.
Less-used functions could be hidden behind menus, a secondary area, or a touchscreen.
A touchscreen could show large visual icons and scale controls for older users.
A TV-screen menu could be used for many options, but this has compatibility concerns.
Primary buttons should remain visible and directly pushable.
Extra functions could appear on a touchscreen or secondary display area.
A docking station could be used for updates or configuration.

expected_actions.action: Take minutes and put updated minutes in the shared folder.
Do another individual-work session after lunch.
Use the next email instructions for the next meeting or work session.
Save the smartboard or session output into shared/project documents, preferably as an image such as JPEG.
Fill out the questionnaire after lunch.

expected_risks: Adding Bluetooth, radio, or a receiver could increase cost and complexity.
Relying on TV-screen menus may not work across all televisions.
Too many visible functions or buttons can make the remote hard to use.
Combining too many functions into one button can confuse users.
A pop-open or flip-open mechanism may be sensitive or easy to break.
A touchscreen or LCD-style design may add cost or implementation complexity.
Focusing too much on younger users may reduce usability for older users.
expected_open_questions: How much teletext should remain if management expects internet to become the main focus.
Whether touchscreen or LCD-style controls are feasible within the cost and compatibility constraints.
Whether extra functions should be on the remote itself or displayed through a TV menu.
How to balance fewer buttons against the desire for originality and multi-function capability.
Whether a remote-locator beep or speech-triggered locator feature is worth exploring.
How to incorporate the company slogan and logo into the remote design.
Whether the lunch break is thirty minutes or forty-five minutes.
resolved_questions: The remote should be TV-only rather than multi-purpose.
The main design direction should keep important buttons visible and easy to use.
The right-hand design option receives the team vote.
The team prefers avoiding a fragile pop-open mechanism.
The team will continue with individual work after lunch.
acceptable_behavior: Summarize this as a functional design meeting for a TV remote.
Capture the degraded audio quality as a fixture attribute, not as meeting content.
Capture concrete decisions, especially TV-only scope, simplicity, infrared direction, primary-button accessibility, and the right-hand design vote.
Capture relative timing such as after lunch and thirty minutes without inventing calendar dates.
Capture explicit actions around minutes, shared folder, individual work, saved session output, and questionnaire.
Treat touchscreen, locator, docking station, and TV-menu ideas as design ideas unless stated as final.
must_not_invent: A final supplier or hardware bill of materials
A final decision to use Bluetooth or radio
A final decision to remove teletext entirely
A final decision that the remote works with every TV
Named people beyond the AMI roles
Facts from outside the first 48-minute clip
